facts.explanation: Montelukast prevents asthma attacks, exercise-induced bronchospasm, and treats allergic rhinitis. It is particularly useful when inhaler adherence is poor. It should not be used as monotherapy for moderate-severe asthma.
facts.wowFactor: Montelukast is one of the few asthma drugs that works for both asthma AND allergies — leukotrienes drive inflammation in both conditions.
facts.statement: Montelukast prevents asthma attacks, exercise-induced bronchospasm, and treats allergic rhinitis.
facts.visualDescription: Runner using montelukast before exercise preventing bronchospasm, allergic rhinitis patient breathing clearly, Singulair pill

facts.quizQuestion: What drug class does diphenhydramine belong to?
facts.explanation: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) is a first-generation H1 antihistamine. Unlike second-generation antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine), it crosses the blood-brain barrier causing sedation. It also has significant anticholinergic effects.
facts.wowFactor: Diphenhydramine is so sedating that it was reformulated into Unisom — the same allergy drug sold separately as a sleep aid.
facts.statement: Diphenhydramine is a first-generation antihistamine that crosses the BBB, causing sedation and anticholinergic effects.
facts.visualDescription: Benadryl tablet, blood-brain barrier with drug crossing it, sedation zzz icon, anticholinergic effects diagram

facts.correctAnswer: Blocks H1 receptors; crosses blood-brain barrier (sedating)
acceptableAlternatives: antagonizes H1 receptors with CNS penetration
distractors: Selectively blocks peripheral H1 receptors without CNS penetration
Blocks dopamine D2 receptors causing EPS reversal
Blocks mast cell calcium channels preventing degranulation
facts.quizQuestion: What is the mechanism of action of diphenhydramine?
facts.explanation: Diphenhydramine competitively blocks H1 histamine receptors. Because it crosses the blood-brain barrier, it blocks both peripheral H1 (allergic response) and central H1 receptors (causing sedation). It also blocks muscarinic receptors (anticholinergic effects).
facts.wowFactor: Diphenhydramine's anticholinergic properties explain why it reverses acute EPS from antipsychotics — blocking muscarinic receptors counteracts dopamine receptor blockade in the basal ganglia.
facts.statement: Diphenhydramine blocks H1 receptors and crosses the BBB, causing both antihistamine and sedating CNS effects.
facts.visualDescription: H1 receptor blocked in peripheral tissue (allergy) and in brain (sedation), BBB crossing arrows, anticholinergic effects listed

acceptableAlternatives: excessive sedation; dry mouth; urinary retention
facts.quizQuestion: What are the major adverse this of diphenhydramine?
facts.explanation: Sedation is the primary adverse effect. Anticholinergic effects include dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, and blurred vision. Paradoxical excitation occurs in children. Diphenhydramine is on the Beers list for older adults — avoid due to fall risk, cognitive impairment, and urinary retention.
facts.wowFactor: Benadryl is one of the most dangerous drugs for the elderly — it causes confusion, falls, and urinary retention, yet is still OTC and taken by many seniors for sleep.
facts.statement: Diphenhydramine causes sedation, anticholinergic effects, and is on the Beers list — avoid in elderly patients.
facts.visualDescription: Elderly patient with fall risk sign, confusion icon, Beers list document, anticholinergic burden diagram

facts.quizQuestion: What are the clinical indications for diphenhydramine?
facts.explanation: Diphenhydramine treats acute allergic reactions, mild insomnia (OTC sleep aid), motion sickness, and is the antidote for acute extrapyramidal symptoms (EPS) from antipsychotics or metoclopramide. It is also used in the anaphylaxis protocol (with epinephrine).
facts.wowFactor: The same antihistamine that treats bee sting allergies is also the antidote for the involuntary muscle spasms caused by antipsychotic medications — three completely different uses, one drug.
facts.statement: Diphenhydramine treats allergic reactions, insomnia, motion sickness, and acute EPS from antipsychotics.
facts.visualDescription: Three-panel: allergy reaction treated, sleep aid for insomnia, antipsychotic EPS reversed with Benadryl IV

facts.quizQuestion: What drug class does theophylline belong to?
facts.explanation: Theophylline (Theo-24) is a methylxanthine bronchodilator — chemically related to caffeine. It is an older drug now used only as an adjunct for asthma or COPD when other treatments fail, due to its very narrow therapeutic index.
facts.wowFactor: Theophylline is essentially a prescription-strength caffeine derivative — which explains why patients experience insomnia, tachycardia, and tremor as toxicity develops.
facts.statement: Theophylline is a methylxanthine bronchodilator with a very narrow therapeutic index, used as adjunct for asthma/COPD.
facts.visualDescription: Methylxanthine chemical structure similar to caffeine, narrow therapeutic window graph, Theo-24 extended-release capsule

distractors: Blocks muscarinic M3 receptors in airway smooth muscle
Blocks leukotriene D4 receptors reducing bronchoconstriction
Suppresses airway inflammation via glucocorticoid receptors
Blocks H1 receptors reducing airway allergic response
Inhibits mast cell degranulation and histamine release
Enhances GABA activity reducing smooth muscle tone
facts.quizQuestion: What is the mechanism of action of theophylline?
facts.explanation: Theophylline inhibits phosphodiesterase (preventing cAMP/cGMP breakdown, causing smooth muscle relaxation) and blocks adenosine receptors (contributing to bronchodilation and CNS stimulation). Both mechanisms contribute to its bronchodilator effect.
facts.wowFactor: Theophylline blocks adenosine receptors — the same mechanism by which caffeine makes you feel awake. This explains why coffee and theophylline together can cause serious cardiac arrhythmias.
facts.statement: Theophylline inhibits phosphodiesterase (raising cAMP) and blocks adenosine receptors, producing bronchodilation.
facts.visualDescription: Phosphodiesterase enzyme blocked, cAMP levels rising, airway smooth muscle relaxing, adenosine receptor blocked with stimulant effects

acceptableAlternatives: narrow therapeutic index toxicity: tachycardia, seizures
facts.quizQuestion: What are the toxic effects of theophylline?
facts.explanation: At therapeutic levels (10-20 mcg/mL), theophylline causes mild GI upset and insomnia. Toxic levels cause tachycardia, dysrhythmias, tremor, nausea/vomiting, and seizures. Cigarette smoking induces CYP1A2, REDUCING theophylline levels (opposite of most drugs).
facts.wowFactor: If a patient on theophylline STOPS smoking, their theophylline levels can jump dangerously high — quitting smoking is actually a drug interaction risk for this medication.
facts.statement: Theophylline toxicity causes tachycardia, seizures, and nausea; therapeutic level 10-20 mcg/mL; smoking decreases levels.
facts.visualDescription: Narrow therapeutic window graph with toxic range above and subtherapeutic below, seizure alert, cigarette with level-changing arrow

facts.correctAnswer: Monitor serum levels (10-20 mcg/mL); avoid caffeine
acceptableAlternatives: monitor drug levels; many drug interactions
distractors: Give 30 min before meals; shake before use
Rinse mouth after every use; avoid in elderly
Monitor INR; hold for bleeding signs
Monitor CBC; report sore throat immediately
facts.quizQuestion: What is the nursing priority for a patient on theophylline?
facts.explanation: Monitor serum theophylline levels (goal 10-20 mcg/mL). Avoid caffeine (additive xanthine effect increases toxicity). Ciprofloxacin and cimetidine increase theophylline levels (inhibit CYP1A2). Smoking decreases levels. Signs of toxicity (tachycardia, nausea) require immediate level check.
facts.wowFactor: A cup of coffee on top of theophylline therapy is not just energizing — it stacks xanthine effects and can push a stable patient into toxicity territory.
facts.statement: Monitor theophylline levels (10-20 mcg/mL), avoid caffeine, watch for tachycardia and GI symptoms as early toxicity signs.
facts.visualDescription: Serum level graph with therapeutic range, caffeine crossed out, ciprofloxacin interaction warning, smoking-level relationship diagram

facts.quizQuestion: What drug class does oxytocin belong to?
facts.explanation: Oxytocin (Pitocin) is a uterotonic (oxytocic) — it stimulates uterine contractions for labor induction/augmentation and prevents postpartum hemorrhage by contracting the uterus after delivery.
facts.wowFactor: Oxytocin is also the brain's bonding hormone — the same molecule that triggers uterine contractions also mediates trust, attachment, and social bonding between humans.
facts.statement: Oxytocin (Pitocin) is a uterotonic that stimulates uterine contractions for labor induction and postpartum hemorrhage prevention.
facts.visualDescription: Uterus with regular contraction waves, Pitocin IV drip, fetal heart monitor, labor and delivery room setting

facts.correctAnswer: Stimulates oxytocin receptors in uterine smooth muscle
acceptableAlternatives: binds myometrial oxytocin receptors causing contractions
distractors: Blocks beta-2 receptors to allow uterine contractions
Inhibits prostaglandin synthesis reducing cervical ripening
Blocks neuromuscular transmission at uterine motor endplates
Stimulates beta-2 receptors relaxing uterine smooth muscle
Prevents maternal Rh sensitization by destroying fetal RBCs
Suppresses ovulation via negative feedback on hypothalamus
Blocks estrogen receptors in uterine tissue
facts.quizQuestion: What is the mechanism of action of the posterior pituitary peptide hormone used to induce or augment labor?
facts.explanation: Oxytocin binds specific G protein-coupled oxytocin receptors on uterine smooth muscle (myometrium). Receptor density increases at term, making the uterus progressively more sensitive. Receptor binding increases intracellular calcium, triggering contractions.
facts.wowFactor: Uterine oxytocin receptors increase 300-fold near term — the body prepares its own sensitivity amplifier so that natural oxytocin surges can trigger labor.
facts.statement: Oxytocin binds myometrial G protein-coupled receptors, increasing intracellular calcium and triggering uterine contractions.
facts.visualDescription: Myometrial cell with oxytocin receptor activation, calcium ion release, uterine smooth muscle contraction wave

facts.quizQuestion: What are the most serious adverse effects of oxytocin?
facts.explanation: Uterine hyperstimulation (too frequent or prolonged contractions) reduces fetal oxygen delivery, causing fetal distress or uterine rupture. Oxytocin has ADH-like activity — high doses cause water retention, hyponatremia, and water intoxication.
facts.wowFactor: Oxytocin is structurally similar to ADH — at high doses it causes water retention so severe that mothers have developed fatal hyponatremia and cerebral edema from Pitocin infusions.
facts.statement: Oxytocin can cause uterine hyperstimulation (fetal distress) and water intoxication (hyponatremia) from ADH-like activity.
facts.visualDescription: Contraction pattern showing tachysystole, fetal heart monitor showing decelerations, sodium lab critically low, IV fluid restriction alert

facts.correctAnswer: Titrate; monitor FHR; stop if hyperstimulation
acceptableAlternatives: discontinue if uterine hyperstimulation; monitor fetal HR
distractors: Give IV push; titrate to maternal pain response
Monitor INR; hold for bleeding signs
Give IM in anterolateral thigh; do not give IV
Monitor CBC; report sore throat immediately
Give on empty stomach 30 min before meals
facts.quizQuestion: What nursing actions are critical during oxytocin administration for labor induction?
facts.explanation: Titrate oxytocin per protocol, monitoring contractions (frequency, duration, intensity) and fetal heart rate continuously. STOP the infusion immediately for uterine hyperstimulation (>5 contractions/10 min, or prolonged contractions >90 sec). Terbutaline is the antidote for hyperstimulation.
facts.wowFactor: The nurse — not the physician — controls the oxytocin pump at the bedside and must make real-time decisions to stop infusion when fetal heart rate decelerations appear.
facts.statement: Titrate oxytocin to contraction pattern; monitor FHR continuously; STOP immediately if uterine hyperstimulation occurs.
facts.visualDescription: Nurse at bedside monitoring contraction monitor and FHR simultaneously, hand on IV pump, stop infusion protocol diagram

facts.quizQuestion: What drug class does magnesium sulfate belong to?
facts.explanation: Magnesium sulfate (MgSO4) serves as an anticonvulsant for preeclampsia/eclampsia and as a tocolytic for preterm labor. It also provides neuroprotection for preterm neonates.
facts.wowFactor: Magnesium sulfate is simultaneously used to prevent maternal seizures AND protect the premature baby's brain — the same electrolyte supplement doing two completely different jobs.
facts.statement: Magnesium sulfate prevents eclamptic seizures and inhibits uterine contractions (tocolytic) in preterm labor.
facts.visualDescription: Pregnant patient on L&D unit, magnesium IV drip, seizure prevention shield, DTR hammer, preterm fetus brain protection icon

facts.correctAnswer: Blocks neuromuscular transmission; reduces ACh release
acceptableAlternatives: inhibits neuromuscular junction by competing with calcium
distractors: Enhances GABA-A receptor activity blocking seizure foci
Blocks sodium channels stabilizing neuronal membranes
Stimulates oxytocin receptors causing uterine relaxation
Blocks beta-2 receptors allowing uterine contraction
Inhibits prostaglandin synthesis at inflammation sites
Competitively blocks mu-opioid receptors in the CNS
Stimulates ADH receptors increasing water reabsorption
facts.quizQuestion: What is the mechanism of action of magnesium sulfate?
facts.explanation: Magnesium competes with calcium at the neuromuscular junction, blocking presynaptic calcium channels and reducing acetylcholine release. This inhibits neuromuscular transmission, relaxing both skeletal muscle (preventing seizures) and uterine smooth muscle (tocolysis).
facts.wowFactor: Magnesium works by being an anti-calcium — it competes with calcium everywhere calcium signals, which is why magnesium toxicity causes everything to shut down progressively.
facts.statement: Magnesium sulfate competes with calcium at the neuromuscular junction, reducing ACh release and inhibiting muscle excitability.
facts.visualDescription: Neuromuscular junction with magnesium ions competing with calcium for presynaptic channels, reduced ACh vesicle release

facts.quizQuestion: What are the most dangerous adverse effects of magnesium sulfate toxicity?
facts.explanation: Progressive magnesium toxicity: first, loss of deep tendon reflexes (DTRs) at 7-10 mEq/L; then respiratory depression at 10-13 mEq/L; cardiac arrest at >15 mEq/L. Therapeutic range: 4-7 mEq/L. DTR loss is the earliest warning sign.
facts.wowFactor: Magnesium toxicity kills in a predictable order: first your reflexes disappear, then you stop breathing, then your heart stops — each stage a warning before the next.
facts.statement: Magnesium toxicity causes DTR loss → respiratory depression → cardiac arrest; therapeutic range 4-7 mEq/L.
facts.visualDescription: Toxicity progression chart: DTR loss (7-10), respiratory depression (10-13), cardiac arrest (>15 mEq/L), calcium gluconate antidote ready

facts.quizQuestion: What is the antidote for magnesium sulfate toxicity?
facts.explanation: Calcium gluconate is the antidote — calcium competes directly with magnesium for the same binding sites. A 10 mL syringe of 10% calcium gluconate must be kept at the bedside of every patient receiving IV magnesium sulfate.
facts.wowFactor: Calcium gluconate must be at the bedside the entire time a patient is on IV magnesium — not in the supply room, not at the nurses' station. At the bedside. Always.
facts.statement: Calcium gluconate (10 mL of 10% solution IV) reverses magnesium sulfate toxicity; must be kept at bedside.
facts.visualDescription: Calcium gluconate syringe prominently placed at patient bedside, IV magnesium running, DTR hammer check, respiratory monitor

facts.quizQuestion: What drug class does terbutaline belong to?
facts.explanation: Terbutaline (Brethine) is a beta-2 adrenergic agonist used as a tocolytic (uterine relaxant). It relaxes uterine smooth muscle to delay preterm delivery and reverse acute uterine hyperstimulation from oxytocin.
facts.wowFactor: Terbutaline is the emergency antidote for oxytocin overdose — when the uterus contracts too much, the beta-2 agonist reverses it by relaxing smooth muscle.
facts.statement: Terbutaline is a beta-2 agonist tocolytic that relaxes uterine smooth muscle to delay preterm labor.
facts.visualDescription: Uterus relaxing with terbutaline, halted contraction waves, preterm labor clock paused, Brethine vial

facts.quizQuestion: What are the major adverse effects of terbutaline?
facts.explanation: Beta-2 stimulation causes tachycardia (some beta-1 crossover), tremor, and hyperglycemia (glycogenolysis). Serious complications include pulmonary edema, especially with IV terbutaline. The Black Box warning prohibits use beyond 48-72 hours or for long-term tocolysis.
facts.wowFactor: Terbutaline has a Black Box warning against use for more than 48-72 hours — prolonged use causes pulmonary edema in pregnant patients at a high enough rate to be fatal.
facts.statement: Terbutaline causes tachycardia, tremor, and hyperglycemia; Black Box prohibits use beyond 48-72 hours due to pulmonary edema risk.
facts.visualDescription: Maternal tachycardia on monitor, tremor icon, blood glucose meter elevated, Black Box warning on Brethine label, 48-hour clock

facts.correctAnswer: Monitor maternal HR and FHR; monitor glucose
acceptableAlternatives: monitor maternal tachycardia; limit to 48-72 hours
distractors: Monitor DTRs every hour; have calcium gluconate at bedside
Titrate to contraction pattern; stop for uterine hyperstimulation
Monitor INR; hold for bleeding signs
Monitor CBC; report sore throat immediately
Give on empty stomach 30 min before meals
Monitor blood glucose every 4 hours only
Taper gradually to prevent adrenal crisis
facts.quizQuestion: What nursing monitoring is required for a patient receiving terbutaline?
facts.explanation: Monitor maternal HR (tachycardia is expected but should not exceed 120 bpm), FHR, blood pressure, and blood glucose. Auscultate lung sounds frequently for pulmonary edema. Use for maximum 48-72 hours per Black Box warning. Monitor for chest pain (pulmonary edema).
facts.wowFactor: Every hour that terbutaline runs past 48 hours increases the risk of pulmonary edema — nurses must advocate for timely reassessment and discontinuation.
facts.statement: Monitor maternal HR, FHR, glucose, and lung sounds; limit terbutaline to 48-72 hours to prevent pulmonary edema.
facts.visualDescription: Maternal HR and FHR monitors, blood glucose meter, lung auscultation, 48-hour timer for Black Box limit

facts.quizQuestion: What are the primary obstetric indications for terbutaline?
facts.explanation: Terbutaline is used for short-term (≤48-72 hours) tocolysis in preterm labor to delay delivery and allow corticosteroids to mature fetal lungs. It is also the emergency treatment for acute uterine hyperstimulation caused by oxytocin.
facts.wowFactor: The goal of terbutaline tocolysis is not to prevent preterm birth entirely — just to buy 48 hours for betamethasone to mature the baby's lungs before delivery.
facts.statement: Terbutaline delays preterm labor (≤48-72 hrs) and reverses acute uterine hyperstimulation from oxytocin.
facts.visualDescription: Preterm labor timeline: terbutaline buys 48 hours, betamethasone lung maturation during window, fetal lung maturity icon

facts.quizQuestion: What drug class does Rho(D) immune globulin belong to?
facts.explanation: Rho(D) immune globulin (RhoGAM) is an immunoglobulin — a concentrated antibody preparation. It prevents Rh sensitization by destroying fetal Rh-positive red blood cells in the maternal circulation before the mother's immune system can recognize and respond to them.
facts.wowFactor: RhoGAM prevents the immune system from ever learning that Rh+ blood exists — without it, a mother's immune system would remember Rh+ blood and attack every future Rh+ baby.
facts.statement: RhoGAM is an immunoglobulin that prevents maternal Rh sensitization by destroying fetal Rh+ RBCs before immune recognition.
facts.visualDescription: Rh-negative mother, fetal-maternal hemorrhage diagram, RhoGAM antibodies destroying fetal Rh+ cells before sensitization, future pregnancy protected

facts.correctAnswer: Destroys fetal Rh+ RBCs in maternal circulation before sensitization
acceptableAlternatives: prevents maternal antibody formation against Rh antigen
distractors: Stimulates maternal B cells to produce Rh antibodies
Blocks Rh receptors on maternal immune cells
Stimulates fetal immune system to tolerate Rh antigens
Destroys Rh-negative fetal RBCs to prevent incompatibility
Suppresses prostaglandin synthesis reducing cervical ripening
Blocks maternal T cells from crossing the placenta
Provides passive immunity against group B streptococcus
facts.quizQuestion: What is the mechanism of action of Rho(D) immune globulin?
facts.explanation: RhoGAM contains anti-D antibodies that bind and destroy any Rh-positive fetal red blood cells entering maternal circulation before the mother's immune system can recognize them as foreign and mount an adaptive immune response (sensitization).
facts.wowFactor: RhoGAM works by out-racing the immune system — it destroys the target (Rh+ cells) before the adaptive immune system can even notice them and build memory antibodies.
facts.statement: RhoGAM's anti-D antibodies destroy fetal Rh+ red cells in maternal blood, preventing maternal sensitization and antibody formation.
facts.visualDescription: Maternal circulation with Rh+ fetal cells, anti-D antibodies from RhoGAM binding and tagging them for destruction, no sensitization occurring

facts.correctAnswer: Give at 28 wks and within 72 hrs postpartum
acceptableAlternatives: administer at 28 weeks and within 72 hours of delivery
distractors: Give at 20 weeks only; not needed at delivery
Give only to Rh-positive mothers at delivery

Give at delivery only if mother is Rh-negative and baby is Rh-negative
Monitor INR; hold for bleeding signs
Monitor CBC; report sore throat immediately
facts.quizQuestion: What is the correct timing and indication for Rho(D) immune globulin?
facts.explanation: Give to Rh-NEGATIVE mothers at 28 weeks gestation (routine prophylaxis) AND within 72 hours of delivery if the newborn is Rh-POSITIVE. Also given after miscarriage, ectopic pregnancy, amniocentesis, or any event causing potential fetal-maternal hemorrhage.
facts.wowFactor: RhoGAM has a 72-hour window after delivery — beyond that, the mother's immune system may already have learned to recognize Rh+ blood, making the drug ineffective.
facts.statement: Give RhoGAM to Rh-negative mothers at 28 weeks and within 72 hours of delivery if the newborn is Rh-positive.
facts.visualDescription: 28-week pregnancy calendar with RhoGAM injection, delivery room with Rh+ newborn test result, 72-hour window clock

distractors: All pregnant women regardless of Rh status
Newborns with hemolytic disease of the fetus
facts.quizQuestion: What is the primary indication for Rho(D) immune globulin?
facts.explanation: RhoGAM is given to Rh-negative mothers at risk of exposure to Rh-positive fetal blood — preventing sensitization that would cause hemolytic disease of the fetus/newborn (HDFN) in future pregnancies.
facts.wowFactor: Before RhoGAM was developed in 1968, Rh incompatibility caused thousands of neonatal deaths each year from hemolytic disease — RhoGAM essentially made this problem preventable.
facts.statement: RhoGAM is indicated for Rh-negative mothers exposed to Rh-positive fetal blood, preventing sensitization and HDFN in future pregnancies.
facts.visualDescription: Rh-negative mother blood type diagram, Rh-positive fetus, RhoGAM preventing sensitization arrow, future healthy Rh+ pregnancy

facts.quizQuestion: What drug class does tamoxifen belong to?
facts.explanation: Tamoxifen (Nolvadex) is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM). It BLOCKS estrogen receptors in breast tissue (anti-estrogenic) but ACTIVATES them in bone and uterus (pro-estrogenic). Used for ER-positive breast cancer.
facts.wowFactor: Tamoxifen is tissue-selective — it is simultaneously anti-estrogen in breast (treating cancer) and pro-estrogen in bone (protecting against osteoporosis) and uterus (increasing cancer risk there).
facts.statement: Tamoxifen is a SERM that blocks estrogen receptors in breast tissue while acting as an estrogen agonist in bone and uterus.
facts.visualDescription: Breast cancer cell with estrogen receptor blocked (anti-tumor), bone cell with estrogen receptor activated (protective), uterus with cancer risk warning

facts.correctAnswer: Blocks estrogen receptors in breast; agonist in bone and uterus
acceptableAlternatives: competitive estrogen receptor antagonist in breast tissue
distractors: Inhibits aromatase enzyme, preventing estrogen synthesis
Destroys estrogen-producing granulosa cells in ovary
Blocks progesterone receptors in breast cancer cells
Stimulates natural killer cells to destroy cancer cells
Inhibits HER2 receptors on breast cancer cells
Prevents estrogen receptor synthesis in all tissues
facts.quizQuestion: What is the mechanism of action of tamoxifen?
facts.explanation: Tamoxifen competes with estradiol for estrogen receptors. In breast tissue, the tamoxifen-receptor complex is inactive — blocking estrogen-driven cancer cell growth. In bone and uterus, the complex has partial agonist activity — protecting bone density but increasing endometrial cancer risk.
facts.wowFactor: Tamoxifen's tissue selectivity comes from how the receptor changes shape when tamoxifen (vs. estrogen) binds it — a small chemical difference creates completely different downstream effects.
facts.statement: Tamoxifen competes with estradiol for receptors; in breast tissue the complex is inactive (cancer-blocking); in uterus it is a partial agonist (cancer risk).
facts.visualDescription: Estrogen receptor with tamoxifen bound in breast (inactive complex), same receptor in uterus (partial agonist activation), cancer growth halted in breast

facts.correctAnswer: Endometrial cancer risk; VTE risk; hot flashes
facts.quizQuestion: What are the major adverse effects of tamoxifen?
facts.explanation: Tamoxifen's estrogen agonist activity in the uterus increases endometrial cancer risk — requiring annual gynecologic exams and monitoring for abnormal vaginal bleeding. VTE (DVT/PE) risk is increased. Hot flashes occur from estrogen antagonism. Ophthalmologic effects (retinopathy) occur with long-term use.
facts.wowFactor: A breast cancer drug that increases endometrial cancer risk — the benefit of preventing breast cancer recurrence must be weighed against a new cancer risk in another reproductive organ.
facts.statement: Tamoxifen increases endometrial cancer and VTE risk; monitor for abnormal vaginal bleeding with annual gynecologic exams.
facts.visualDescription: Uterine lining with cancer risk warning, DVT deep vein diagram, hot flash symptom, annual gynecologic exam calendar

facts.correctAnswer: Annual gyn exam; monitor for vaginal bleeding
acceptableAlternatives: monitor for endometrial cancer signs; annual gynecology
distractors: Monitor INR weekly; avoid vitamin K-rich foods
Hold if HR < 60; do not stop abruptly
Monitor CBC; report sore throat immediately
Give on empty stomach 30 min before breakfast
Give IV push; monitor for QT prolongation
Remain upright 30 min after each dose
facts.quizQuestion: What nursing interventions are essential for a patient on tamoxifen?
facts.explanation: Annual gynecologic examination is required to screen for endometrial cancer. Teach patients to report any abnormal vaginal bleeding immediately. Counsel on increased VTE risk — avoid prolonged immobility. Annual ophthalmologic exam for retinopathy with long-term use.
facts.wowFactor: Abnormal vaginal bleeding in a postmenopausal woman on tamoxifen is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise — immediate reporting is critical.
facts.statement: Annual gyn exam required on tamoxifen; report any abnormal vaginal bleeding immediately; counsel on VTE risk.
facts.visualDescription: Annual exam calendar, vaginal bleeding alert card, DVT prevention stockings, ophthalmology eye exam for long-term users

facts.quizQuestion: The pill containing both estrogen and progestin to prevent pregnancy belongs to which drug class?
facts.explanation: Combined oral contraceptives (Ortho-Cyclen, Yaz) contain synthetic estrogen (ethinyl estradiol) and progestin. They suppress ovulation via negative feedback on the hypothalamus and pituitary, and have additional mechanisms (thickened cervical mucus, thinned endometrium).
facts.wowFactor: The pill works by convincing the brain that the woman is already pregnant — high hormone levels suppress the FSH and LH surge needed for ovulation.
facts.statement: Combined oral contraceptives contain estrogen and progestin that suppress ovulation via hypothalamic-pituitary feedback inhibition.
facts.visualDescription: Pill pack (estrogen and progestin), hypothalamic-pituitary axis with suppressed FSH and LH, no ovulation diagram

facts.correctAnswer: Suppresses ovulation via negative feedback on hypothalamic-pituitary axis
acceptableAlternatives: inhibits GnRH and LH/FSH suppressing ovulation
distractors: Destroys Rh-positive fetal red blood cells
Blocks estrogen receptors in reproductive tissue
Blocks sperm motility via direct cervical penetration
Inhibits follicular development via LH surge amplification
Activates progesterone receptors causing endometrial thickening
Blocks beta-2 receptors in uterine smooth muscle
facts.quizQuestion: What is the mechanism of action of combined oral contraceptives?
facts.explanation: Combined OCs deliver constant exogenous estrogen and progestin, which suppress GnRH (from the hypothalamus) and FSH/LH (from the pituitary) via negative feedback. Without the LH surge, ovulation cannot occur. Additional effects: thickened cervical mucus and thinner endometrium.
facts.wowFactor: The pill has three redundant mechanisms — suppressing ovulation, blocking sperm with thick cervical mucus, AND preventing implantation with thin endometrium — making it >99% effective when taken correctly.
facts.statement: Combined OCs suppress FSH/LH via negative feedback, preventing the LH surge needed for ovulation.
facts.visualDescription: Hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis with exogenous estrogen/progestin suppressing FSH/LH, no LH surge, ovulation absent

facts.quizQuestion: What are the major adverse effects of combined oral contraceptives?
facts.explanation: Estrogen increases clotting factor production, raising VTE risk (DVT, PE). Other effects: hypertension (common), nausea, breast tenderness, headache, and breakthrough bleeding. CONTRAINDICATED in smokers >35 years old, history of VTE, migraine with aura, and breast cancer.
facts.wowFactor: The VTE risk from combined OCs is real but context-dependent: it is lower than the VTE risk of pregnancy itself — the risk of NOT using contraception may actually be higher.
facts.statement: Combined OCs increase VTE and hypertension risk; contraindicated in smokers >35, history of VTE, or migraine with aura.
facts.visualDescription: DVT deep vein clot diagram, BP cuff showing elevated reading, contraindication list: smoker >35, VTE history, migraine with aura

facts.quizQuestion: Which drug most significantly this the effectiveness of combined oral contraceptives?
facts.explanation: Rifampin is a potent CYP3A4 inducer — it dramatically accelerates the metabolism of ethinyl estradiol and progestin, reducing their plasma concentrations and contraceptive effectiveness. Patients must use backup contraception. Anticonvulsants (phenytoin, carbamazepine) also reduce OC efficacy.
facts.wowFactor: A tuberculosis drug (rifampin) can cause an unintended pregnancy — nurses must counsel any woman on rifampin to use a backup contraceptive method immediately.
facts.statement: Rifampin induces CYP3A4, accelerating OC metabolism and reducing contraceptive effectiveness; backup method required.
facts.visualDescription: Rifampin pill with CYP3A4 enzyme inducing rapid OC breakdown, contraceptive effectiveness dropping, backup contraception sign

facts.correctAnswer: Same time daily; avoid in smokers over 35
acceptableAlternatives: same time every day; smokers over 35 contraindicated
distractors: Take on empty stomach 30 min before meals only
Hold 48 hours before iodinated contrast procedures
Monitor INR weekly; avoid vitamin K-rich foods
Monitor CBC; report sore throat immediately
Give IV push; monitor for QT prolongation
Remain upright 30 min after each dose
Monitor blood glucose every 4 hours; rotate injection sites
facts.quizQuestion: What nursing education points are most important for a patient starting combined oral contraceptives?
facts.explanation: Take at the SAME TIME every day (consistency maintains hormone levels). Teach ACHES warning signs: Abdominal pain (DVT/PE), Chest pain (MI/PE), Headache (stroke), Eye changes (stroke), Severe leg pain (DVT). Contraindicated in smokers >35 (stroke risk). Many drug interactions reduce efficacy.
facts.wowFactor: The mnemonic ACHES — Abdominal pain, Chest pain, Headache, Eye changes, Severe leg pain — helps patients identify the serious thrombotic complications that require immediate medical attention.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the mechanism of action of docusate?
facts.explanation: Docusate is an anionic surfactant. It lowers the surface tension of the fecal mass, allowing water and fat to mix into stool and soften it. This is a purely physical mechanism — it does not stimulate peristalsis.
facts.wowFactor: Docusate works exactly like dish soap — reducing surface tension so water penetrates the stool, the same chemistry that lets soap cut through grease.
facts.statement: Docusate acts as a surfactant, reducing fecal surface tension so water and fat penetrate and soften stool.

facts.quizQuestion: What are the primary indications for ipratropium?
facts.explanation: Ipratropium is first-line for COPD maintenance (works well in the cholinergic-dominant COPD airways) and is used adjunctively with albuterol in severe asthma exacerbations (combined in DuoNeb). It is not a rescue inhaler.
facts.wowFactor: COPD airways have relatively more cholinergic tone than asthma airways — which is exactly why anticholinergics like ipratropium work better for COPD maintenance than for asthma.
facts.statement: Ipratropium is the first-line inhaled bronchodilator for COPD maintenance and adjunct therapy in severe asthma.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the mechanism of action of loperamide?
facts.explanation: Loperamide activates mu-opioid receptors in the enteric nervous system, slowing intestinal peristalsis and reducing fluid secretion. Its P-glycoprotein efflux and poor BBB penetration prevent CNS opioid effects at therapeutic doses.
facts.wowFactor: Loperamide is an opioid that P-glycoprotein actively pumps out of the brain — a cleverly engineered antidiarrheal that cannot cause CNS opioid effects.
facts.statement: Loperamide activates enteric mu-opioid receptors to slow intestinal motility and reduce fluid secretion without CNS effects.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the most serious adverse effect of high-dose pancrelipase?
facts.explanation: GI upset (nausea, abdominal pain, diarrhea) is common. The most serious adverse effect is fibrosing colonopathy — colonic strictures seen primarily in cystic fibrosis patients on very high lipase doses (>10,000 units/kg/day).
facts.wowFactor: High-dose pancrelipase can scar the colon — the very drug that keeps cystic fibrosis patients nourished can harm their gut if dosed excessively.
facts.statement: Pancrelipase causes GI upset; fibrosing colonopathy is a serious risk at very high doses in cystic fibrosis patients.

facts.quizQuestion: What are the primary indications for sulfasalazine?
facts.explanation: Sulfasalazine treats inflammatory bowel disease (ulcerative colitis and Crohn disease) via local 5-ASA anti-inflammatory activity in the colon. It is also used as a DMARD for rheumatoid arthritis via systemic sulfapyridine effects.
facts.wowFactor: One drug, two completely different diseases — 5-ASA treats gut inflammation in IBD, while the sulfapyridine component travels systemically to suppress joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis.
facts.statement: Sulfasalazine treats ulcerative colitis, Crohn disease, and rheumatoid arthritis via its two active components.
